title: [Feature]: Add priority-aware processing lanes
problem: nats-sinks can store priority metadata, but processing currently treats batches uniformly. Some deployments may need controlled preference for urgent or high-priority events during backlog drain, while still preventing starvation and preserving commit-then-acknowledge semantics.
proposal: Research and design priority-aware processing lanes that can select batches by configured subject patterns or metadata priority. The feature should avoid claiming global ordering guarantees and should include starvation controls, bounded in-flight work, and clear metrics for lane utilization.
users: Operators, mission-support teams, platform engineers, and maintainers who need predictable backlog draining under mixed-priority workloads.
delivery_semantics: Priority must not cause ACK before durable success. Priority-aware scheduling may change which messages are fetched or processed first, but every message must still pass validation, sink write, commit, and ACK-last ordering.
security: Priority can be attacker-controlled if accepted from untrusted headers or payloads. The design must validate and bound values, support trusted defaulting, and avoid letting malformed or spoofed priority values bypass policy checks.
acceptance: A design document explains lane behavior, ordering limitations, and starvation controls.
Priority values can be validated and defaulted globally or by subject pattern.
Metrics expose lane counts without leaking sensitive subject detail by default.
Tests cover priority spoofing, missing priority, mixed batches, and starvation prevention.
Documentation warns that priority lanes do not provide exactly-once or strict total ordering.
tests: Add scheduler unit tests, malformed priority tests, subject-aware defaulting tests, metrics tests, and commit-then-ack ordering tests. Add load tests if implementation proceeds beyond design.
documentation: Update docs/configuration.md, docs/architecture.md, docs/metrics.md, docs/operations.md, docs/use-cases, ROADMAP.md, and CHANGELOG.md.
closeout: Close after the release containing design or implementation is published. Include release tag, scheduling behavior, tests run, and known ordering limitations.
